schema.levels: object with keys 1, 2, 3; each level contains exactly three required main quests

intro.title: Meet HTML & CSS
intro.description: Study an introductory reference for HTML & CSS, then summarize what it is, when it is used, and one small practical action a beginner can try.

1.title: Identify HTML & CSS Basics
1.description: Read the beginner material for HTML & CSS, then explain the core terms a new teammate must understand before using it.

1.title: Map a Simple HTML & CSS Use Case
1.description: Describe a small real-world situation where HTML & CSS helps, including the input, action, and expected result.

1.title: Avoid a Common HTML & CSS Mistake
1.description: Find one beginner mistake related to HTML & CSS, then summarize how to notice it and how to avoid it.

2.title: Apply HTML & CSS in Context
2.description: Use the reference to design a practical workflow for HTML & CSS, then explain why each step is needed.

2.title: Compare HTML & CSS Tradeoffs
2.description: Write a comparison between a simple approach and a more structured approach for HTML & CSS, including when each is appropriate.

2.title: Troubleshoot HTML & CSS
2.description: Create a short troubleshooting checklist for HTML & CSS, focusing on symptoms, likely causes, and safe next steps.

3.title: Design a Reusable HTML & CSS Practice
3.description: Turn HTML & CSS into a repeatable practice that another teammate could follow, including review points and quality checks.

3.title: Review HTML & CSS Like a Senior
3.description: Audit a HTML & CSS decision for correctness, maintainability, and risk, then summarize what you would approve or change.

3.title: Prove HTML & CSS Mastery
3.description: Create a small proof-of-understanding artifact for HTML & CSS, such as notes, a checklist, a mini demo, or a before-and-after explanation. Show how document structure and styling combine into usable web pages.

2.title: Apply JavaScript in Context

intro.title: Meet React as a UI Talent
intro.description: Read the React quick start, then summarize what problem React solves and what a component represents.

1.title: Think in Components
1.description: Study how UI is broken into components, then summarize how component boundaries make an interface easier to reason about.

1.title: Describe UI with JSX
1.description: Review how React describes UI, then write the difference between markup structure and component logic in your own words.

1.title: Handle Basic Interaction
1.description: Study event handling and state basics, then summarize how user actions can change what appears on screen.

2.title: Manage State Deliberately
2.description: Read about state management, then explain how deciding where state lives affects component clarity and bugs.

2.title: Reuse Logic with Hooks
2.description: Study custom hooks, then summarize when extracting a hook makes code clearer and when it only hides simple logic.

links.label: React - Reusing Logic with Custom Hooks

2.title: Share Data Through Context
2.description: Learn context usage, then describe a case where context is helpful and a case where passing props is still simpler.

links.label: React - Passing Data Deeply with Context

3.title: Scale State with Reducer and Context
3.description: Study reducer and context scaling, then summarize how a larger UI can organize complex state changes without losing traceability.

links.label: React - Scaling Up with Reducer and Context

3.title: Use Escape Hatches Carefully
3.description: Review escape hatches, then explain why effects and refs should solve integration needs rather than replace normal React data flow.

3.title: Read the API Reference Strategically
3.description: Use the React reference to investigate one API, then write how official API docs help verify behavior beyond tutorials.
